evidence_quotes.quote: I have had numerous games, that I owned, which had their servers taken down
forcing the consumer to send data, telemetry to their databases or become training data for their A.I. models
where they sometimes keep running in the background doing who-knows what, sending anything they wish to their servers without my knowledge
Adobe in this case has a anti-piracy scanning utility that always runs on your computer.
malicious Contract-Cancellation fees or difficult to unsubscribe from plans.
There have also been false promises, that were never delivered to us as well.

paragraphs.text: I'd like to give feedback on this matter and share my negative experiences in terms of how video games and certain software licenses are handled in support of the Digital Fairness Act. I have had numerous games, that I owned, which had their servers taken down, where community efforts of making these games last have been ignored or entirely dismissed, from which the value people have spent inside these games has been basically entirely erased or left inaccessible. I have bought Overwatch 1 back in 2017 for 50 euros. The company decided to entirely remodel the game, remove a lot of options I basically paid for and made a lot of the first iteration's content unattainable for me and many others. In addition to that, there have also been false promises, that were never delivered to us as well. Features that were supposed to make the transformation feel justified. Not delivering these, instead left a sour taste and a feeling of having your money taken away for the sake of them earning more, giving us as consumers less. There are a lot more cases like these, all documented and rich with history of people losing out on their positive experiences with these games or software or have their money lose its worth. I think its unfair making us invest or pay for access to these products only to take it away some time later. There needs to be a way to protect what we rightfully attain. Have a way to continue running servers privately or give the software the means to run offline and have the entirety of the content attainable after the support ends. On the topic of software licenses (as mentioned at the beginning), I bought Adobe Licenses on numerous products of theirs only to be met with malicious Contract-Cancellation fees or difficult to unsubscribe from plans. These fees are insanely expensive and make the missing out on cancelling the trial period they tend to offer, very dangerous and scary to deal with. In my opinion that is not how software, that is made to help people, supposed to be. There is also a lot of stories everywhere about how Adobe has used this to maliciously take more money for not being happy with their software or not wanting to continue supporting them. There is also the case of, for example, buying Photoshop back in the 2000s, expecting the software to stay the way it is, expect improvement, but instead being forced to update it to a state where they are forcing the consumer to send data, telemetry to their databases or become training data for their A.I. models these days instead of having the option to download older versions of their software to not be forced to deal with their anti-privacy practices, which you can not turn off or remove easily without technological know-how on how to manipulate registry, network routing to stop the upload of private data or edit the files on your system. Either that or make it extremely difficult to remove every piece of data they leave on your computer well after having stopped using their software. The same applies to certain games and their anti-cheat systems, where they sometimes keep running in the background doing who-knows what, sending anything they wish to their servers without my knowledge or mask it with "necessity" in their EULA or TOS instead of elaborating what exactly they are doing to my system. Adobe in this case has a anti-piracy scanning utility that always runs on your computer. While anti-piracy is not a bad thing inherently, it still cannot be understated how it can always create holes in my own system without my knowledge or full agreement. These things need to be made obvious, clarified, easily turned-off and most importantly, made with fairness to the consumer in mind. I want to be treated like a buyer of a piece of software for my own benefit and delightfully pay for good service instead of being treated like disposable product myself. This sentiment applies to any piece of software, be it games or work-related studio applications.
